original_abstract:
This article examines the literary and theological function of space in Daniel 1, exploring how spatial references govern the narrative’s ideological structure. By employing a socio-historic and cognitive linguistic framework, the study analyzes the fundamental tension between Jerusalem and Babylon (Shinar). This geographical contrast serves as a proxy for a broader power play between the defeated Judean nation and the ascendant Babylonian Empire, as well as their respective deities.

The author identifies several critical spatial dimensions within the text: the spatio-religious tension of holy vs. profane lands, the social shift from deportation to the royal court, and the mantic wisdom associated with these settings. A central focus is placed on the "rite of passage" experienced by Daniel and his companions—including their renaming and re-education—and the subsequent strategy they employ to maintain their religious identity. By refusing the king’s food and choosing a distinct diet, the protagonists create a symbolic personal space that preserves their loyalty to YHWH within a hostile, foreign environment. The analysis concludes that the narrator utilizes these spatial categories to demonstrate God’s sovereignty, suggesting that even in exile, the God of Israel remains the determinative force behind history and political shifts.
humanized_abstract:
In this article, the function of space in Daniel 1 is investigated. From a literary and theological perspective, it is examined in what way the use of spatial terms structures the ideology of the book of Daniel. The article uses a combination of methods from the field of socio-historic text research and cognitive linguistics, in order to elucidate the conflict between Jerusalem and Shinar. This conflict can be seen as an allegory of the struggle between the conquered Judean people and the ruling power of the empire, but also as an ideological confrontation between the God of the exiled Judeans and the Babylonian patron divinity.

In this article the author examines several spatial dimensions which are mentioned in the text: the spatio-religious tension between holy and profane lands, the social transition from deportation to the royal court and the mantic wisdom related to these places. As the main focus is on the rite of passage of Daniel and his friends who are given new names and educated in wisdom, the author also examines their strategy of survival as God-fearing Israelites in a strange land. Through their refusal of the king’s food and their adoption of a different diet they establish a personal space where they can remain loyal to YHWH. The narrator uses the spatial categories in the text to show God’s sovereignty over historical and political events. It is clear that even in exile the God of Israel is the determining factor.
